To address the above issues, we propose a dynamic model splitting framework (DMSF) for edge-cloud collaborative inference. We design switchable compress–recover modules after each layer, enabling a single model to support all split points and significantly reduce memory overhead. The optimal split point is selected based on bandwidth and edge computational capacity, and the corresponding compress–recover path is activated for fast switching. For FPN-based multi-branch models, we remove the multi-scale branches from the backbone to the FPN and present hierarchical compensation modules in DMSF, making the model more suitable for splitting while maintaining perception accuracy. Experimental results show that DMSF reduces memory overhead by 75%, achieves millisecond-level switching, and outperforms the existing method with 32.0% lower latency and 12.2% higher perception accuracy.

evidence.title: Systematic reconstruction of molecular pathway signatures using scalable single-cell perturbation screens
evidence.abstract: Recent advancements in functional genomics have provided an unprecedented ability to measure diverse molecular modalities, but learning causal regulatory relationships from observational data remains challenging. Here, we leverage pooled genetic screens and single cell sequencing (i.e. Perturb-seq) to systematically identify the targets of signaling regulators in diverse biological contexts. We demonstrate how Perturb-seq is compatible with recent and commercially available advances in combinatorial indexing and next-generation sequencing, and perform more than 1,500 perturbations split across six cell lines and five biological signaling contexts. We introduce an improved computational framework (Mixscale) to address cellular variation in perturbation efficiency, alongside optimized statistical methods to learn differentially expressed gene lists and conserved molecular signatures. Finally, we demonstrate how our Perturb-seq derived gene lists can be used to precisely infer changes in signaling pathway activation for in-vivo and in-situ samples. Our work enhances our understanding of signaling regulators and their targets, and lays a computational framework towards the data-driven inference of an ‘atlas’ of perturbation signatures.

evidence.title: CausalRCA: Causal Inference based Precise Fine-grained Root Cause Localization for Microservice Applications
evidence.abstract: Effectively localizing root causes of performance anomalies is crucial to enabling the rapid recovery and loss mitigation of microservice applications in the cloud. Depending on the granularity of the causes that can be localized, a service operator may take different actions, e.g., restarting or migrating services if only faulty services can be localized (namely, coarse-grained) or scaling resources if specific indicative metrics on the faulty service can be localized (namely, fine-grained). Prior research mainly focuses on coarse-grained faulty service localization, and there is now a growing interest in fine-grained root cause localization to identify faulty services and metrics. Causal inference (CI) based methods have gained popularity recently for root cause localization, but currently used CI methods have limitations, such as the linear causal relations assumption and strict data distribution requirements. To tackle these challenges, we propose a framework named CausalRCA to implement fine-grained, automated, and real-time root cause localization. The CausalRCA uses a gradient-based causal structure learning method to generate weighted causal graphs and a root cause inference method to localize root cause metrics. We conduct coarse- and fine-grained root cause localization to evaluate the localization performance of CausalRCA. Experimental results show that CausalRCA has significantly outperformed baseline methods in localization accuracy, e.g., the average AC@3 of the fine-grained root cause metric localization in the faulty service is 0.719, and the average increase is 10% compared with baseline methods. In addition, the average Avg@5 has improved by 9.43%.

evidence.title: Advanced Stock Prediction with Amazon Cloud
evidence.venue: International Conference on Computing and Convergence Technology
evidence.abstract: For precise stock price forecasts, sophisticated methods are required due to the financial market's volatility and massive data inflow. The research offers a scalable, secure, and effective end-to-end stock prediction system that makes use of Amazon Web Services (AWS) cloud resources. The system incorporates six essential AWS services: AWS Lambda, Amazon SageMaker, Amazon API Gateway, Amazon S3, Amazon CloudWatch, and AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM)-all of which make use of machine learning and serverless computing. The core component of the design is a prediction model that uses XGBoost and is hosted on Amazon SageMaker. Model is ‘trained using historical stock data. The computational layer is A WS Lambda, which communicates with SageMaker to trigger predictions in real time. While Amazon S3 guarantees dependable storage for datasets and model outputs, Amazon API Gateway offers a safe and convenient REST API for client interactions. Amazon CloudWatch is used to manage system monitoring and performance data, while A WS IAM provides strong access control. Underpinned by SageMaker's managed capabilities for model training and deployment, the research work uses cutting-edge machine learning approaches for trend analysis and prediction. With serverless computing allowing for dynamic scalability to accommodate varying workloads in financial markets, this system is built to handle real-time inference demands. The study shows how cloud-based infrastructure may simplify financial analytics by fusing accurate real-time predictions with economical resource management with this AWS-driven solution. Financial institutions, researchers, and traders may all benefit from this scalable and dependable architecture, which offers practical insights to help them deal with the intricacies of the stock market.

evidence.title: TinyFusionDet: Hardware-Efficient LiDAR-Camera Fusion Framework for 3D Object Detection at Edge
evidence.venue: IEEE transactions on circuits and systems for video technology (Print)
evidence.abstract: Current LiDAR-Camera fusion methods for 3D object detection achieve considerable accuracy at the immense cost of computation and storage, posing challenges for the deployment at the edge. To address this issue, we propose a lightweight 3D object detection framework, namely TinyFusionDet. Specially, we put forward an ingenious Hybrid Scale Pillar Strategy in LiDAR point cloud feature extraction to efficiently improve the detection accuracy of small objects. Meanwhile, a low cost Cross-Modal Heatmap Attention module is presented to suppress background interference in image features for reducing false positives. Moreover, a Cross-Modal Feature Interaction module is designed to enhance the cross-modal information fusion among channels for further promoting the detection precision. Extensive experiments demonstrated that TinyFusionDet achieves competitive accuracy with the lowest memory consumption and inference latency, making it suitable for hardware constrained edge devices. Furthermore, TinyFusionDet is implemented on a customized FPGA-based prototype system, yielding a record high energy efficiency up to 114.97GOPS/W. To the best of our knowledge, this marks the first real-time LiDAR-Camera fusion detection framework for edge applications.

evidence.title: Multiscale Channel Attention and Cross-Layer Fusion Network for Infrared Small Target Detection
evidence.venue: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
evidence.abstract: Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) faces challenges due to limited global perception and feature ambiguity in complex scenarios. To address these issues, we propose a novel multiscale channel attention and cross-layer fusion network (MACFNet). The framework integrates three key innovations: 1)the feature convolution attention transformer (FCAT) addresses limited global perception by combining local features and global contexts to enhance target representation; 2) the efficient channel and spatial attention (ECSA) module resolves feature ambiguity by optimizing discriminative feature weighting; and 3) an enhanced M-UNet architecture incorporates channelwise cross fusion transformer (CCT) modules to enable effective cross-scale semantic alignment. Extensive experiments on the SIRST and NUDT-SIRST datasets demonstrate the state-of-the-art performance, achieving significantly higher IoU of 0.8396 and 0.9346, respectively, surpassing the existing model-driven and data-driven methods while maintaining a real-time capable inference speed of 29.7167 FPS.

evidence.title: CamGeo: Sparse Camera-Conditioned Image-to-Video Generation with 3D Geometry Priors
evidence.abstract: Sparse camera-conditioned image-to-video generation presents a pivotal challenge: synthesizing geometrically consistent 3D motion from minimal pose cues. Existing methods, which largely rely on dense supervision or naive interpolation, suffer from severe pose drift and motion discontinuities due to the lack of robust 3D priors. In this paper, we introduce CamGeo, a novel framework that distills rich 3D geometric knowledge from a pre-trained video-to-3D model (VGGT) directly into the diffusion backbone. To achieve this without incurring inference latency, we propose a training-only distillation strategy. Specifically, CamGeo incorporates: (1) keyframe trajectory distillation that enforces cycle-consistency with sparse input poses, (2) cross-frame consistency distillation with both camera trajectory and depth constraints to generate consistent structure across unsupervised frames, and (3) a three-stage coarse-to-fine curriculum learning, progressively scales geometric complexity, from global structure coherence to fine-grained refinement, achieving stable optimization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CamGeo achieves consistent improvements under various sparsity ratios.

evidence.title: Fail-Safe Multi-Robot Awareness During GPS Outages Using CNN-Based LiDAR
evidence.venue: Computer Assisted Radiology and Surgery - International Congress and Exhibition
evidence.abstract: Multi-robot systems deployed in warehouses and indoor facilities are highly vulnerable when GPS and communication networks fail, leading to a loss of situational awareness and an increased risk of collision. To address this question, a fail-safe detection framework is proposed that relies solely on 2D LiDAR sensing and a lightweight convolutional neural network (CNN). The method formulates robot-to-robot detection as a binary segmentation task, in which each LiDAR point is classified as either robot or background. A structured pipeline is employed that integrates distance normalization, CNN inference, clustering, and confidence estimation to provide reliable peer localization. Training data are generated through simulation in warehouse-scale environments with varying layouts, occlusions, and robot densities, ensuring robustness without the need for costly data collection. Experimental validation demonstrates that the proposed framework can maintain accurate robot detection and smooth transitions between GPS-available and GPS-denied modes, while operating in real time on resource-constrained platforms. These results suggest that CNN-based LiDAR segmentation can serve as a practical and fail-safe backup for multi-robot awareness in environments where positioning infrastructure is unreliable or unavailable.

evidence.title: Deep Learning-Based Tunnel Structural Health Monitoring and Automatic Crack Recognition Algorithm
evidence.venue: 2025 2nd International Conference on Software, Systems and Information Technology (SSITCON)
evidence.abstract: Tunnel structures are prone to defects such as cracks during long-term service. Manual inspection methods suffer from low efficiency and are difficult to implement in highrisk environments. To address this issue, this study proposes a data preprocessing strategy that integrates frequency-domain decomposition and an attention mechanism. First, a lightweight two-stream deep network model is constructed based on a modified YOLOv5 (You Only Look Once version 5) architecture. This model innovatively adopts a multi-scale Feature Pyramid Network++ (FPN++) structure and introduces a channel-spatial dual attention mechanism. Finally, a recognition optimization module is designed. Experimental results show that the proposed method exhibits significant advantages in metrics such as recall in complex environments. Deployed on a Jetson Xavier NX platform using TensorRT, the proposed method achieves an inference frame rate of 42.8 FPS, consumes 19.7 W of power, and maintains latency within 23.4 ms. This research provides a new technical solution for efficient crack detection in complex tunnel environments, laying the foundation for the practical application of intelligent tunnel monitoring systems.

evidence.title: The AI Scientist: Towards Fully Automated Open-Ended Scientific Discovery
evidence.abstract: Introduces an automated scientific discovery pipeline that generates ideas, writes code, runs experiments, and drafts papers, while also exposing limits in evaluation and reliability.

evidence.title: The AI Scientist-v2: Workshop-Level Automated Scientific Discovery via Agentic Tree Search
evidence.abstract: Extends automated scientific discovery with stronger search and review-like iteration, motivating stricter gates for novelty, experiment quality, and paper readiness.

evidence.title: Agent Laboratory: Using LLM Agents as Research Assistants
evidence.abstract: Describes agentic research assistance across literature review, experimentation, and report writing, highlighting the need for human oversight and reproducibility controls.

evidence.title: MLE-bench: Evaluating Machine Learning Agents on Machine Learning Engineering
evidence.abstract: Benchmarks agents on machine learning engineering tasks and provides a reference point for evaluating experiment-running ability and reproducibility.

evidence.title: AIBuildAI-2: Scalable Agentic AI Research and Engineering
evidence.abstract: Frames AI systems that build and improve AI systems, including multi-agent research and engineering loops with traceability requirements.

evidence.title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning
evidence.abstract: Uses verbal feedback and memory to help agents improve across trials, motivating explicit failure memory and revision loops.

evidence.title: Self-Refine: Iterative Refinement with Self-Feedback
evidence.abstract: Studies iterative generation, feedback, and refinement, providing background for reviewer-style critique and revision policies.

evidence.title: Voyager: An Open-Ended Embodied Agent with Large Language Models
evidence.abstract: Demonstrates open-ended skill acquisition with memory and iterative exploration, relevant to long-running agent loops and durable lessons.

evidence.title: AgentBench: Evaluating LLMs as Agents
evidence.abstract: Introduces agent evaluation tasks and motivates structured benchmarks for agent behavior rather than isolated text quality.

evidence.title: SWE-agent: Agent-Computer Interfaces Enable Automated Software Engineering
evidence.abstract: Shows how agents can operate computer interfaces to solve software tasks, relevant to local experiment execution and reproducible command logs.
